items.kana: とうきょうにいつかかんいます
english: I'll be in Tokyo for five days
items.note: 〜日間 (〜nichi-kan) = for ~ days. 1日間 (ichinichi-kan), 3日間 (mikkakan), 5日間 (itsukakan), 1週間 (isshuukan) = one week. Template: 「[city] に [duration] います」= I'll be in [city] for [duration]. 「旅行で来ました」= I came for travel/tourism. 「観光で来ています」= I'm here as a tourist. Both are useful when chatting with locals who ask what brings you to Japan.

items.kana: にほんははじめてです
english: It's my first time in Japan
items.note: 初めて (hajimete) = for the first time. 「日本は初めてですか？」= Is this your first time in Japan? → 「はい、初めてです！」= Yes, it's my first time! This naturally opens a warm conversation. Japanese people love helping first-timers and will often offer unsolicited (but excellent) recommendations. Following up with 「とても楽しみにしていました！」= I've been really looking forward to it!

items.script: おすすめの食べ物はありますか
items.kana: おすすめのたべものはありますか
romaji: osusume no tabemono wa arimasu ka
items.note: おすすめ = recommendation, from 薦める (susumeru) = to recommend. Template: 「おすすめの[noun]はありますか？」= Are there any recommended [nouns]? Swap in: 食べ物 (food), レストラン (restaurant), お店 (shop), 観光スポット (tourist spot), アクティビティ. After getting a recommendation, say 「どこで食べられますか？」= Where can I eat it? or 「どこで買えますか？」= Where can I buy it?

items.script: おすすめのレストランを教えてもらえますか
items.kana: おすすめのれすとらんをおしえてもらえますか
romaji: osusume no resutoran wo oshiete moraemasu ka
english: Could you tell me a recommended restaurant?
items.note: 教えてもらえますか = 'could you tell me?'. 教える (oshieru) = to teach/tell. もらえますか = could I receive? → softens into a polite request. More casual: 「おすすめのレストランはどこですか？」= Where is a good restaurant? The politest form: 「おすすめのレストランを教えていただけますか？」(いただく instead of もらう). Hotel concierges and tourist information offices (観光案内所, kankō annai-jo) are excellent for this question.

items.script: おすすめの観光スポットはありますか
items.kana: おすすめのかんこうすぽっとはありますか
romaji: osusume no kankou supotto wa arimasu ka
english: Are there any recommended tourist spots?
items.note: 観光 (kankou) = sightseeing/tourism. スポット (supotto) = spot (from English). 観光スポット = tourist spot. Also useful: 「この辺で何かおすすめはありますか？」= Is there anything recommended around here? 「定番の観光地はどこですか？」= What are the classic tourist destinations? 「穴場はありますか？」= Are there any hidden gems? (穴場 = 'hole place' = off-the-beaten-path spot — locals love being asked this!)

items.script: 何かおすすめのアクティビティはありますか
items.kana: なにかおすすめのあくてぃびてぃはありますか
romaji: nanika osusume no akutibiti wa arimasu ka
items.note: 何か (nanika) = something / anything. Makes the question more open-ended: 'Is there anything at all you'd recommend?' Good follow-ups: 「体験できることはありますか？」= Are there any hands-on experiences? 「茶道体験はできますか？」= Can I do a tea ceremony experience? 「着物を着ることはできますか？」= Can I wear a kimono? 体験 (taiken) = experience (hands-on). Japan has incredible immersive experiences: pottery, calligraphy, cooking classes, samurai sword, etc.

items.kana: よやくはひつようですか
items.note: 予約 (yoyaku) = reservation/booking. 必要 (hitsuyou) = necessary. 「予約なしで行けますか？」= Can I go without a reservation? For popular Tokyo restaurants, the answer is often yes — many places are notoriously hard to book. Useful follow-up: 「どこで予約できますか？」= Where can I make a reservation? 「電話で予約できますか？」= Can I reserve by phone? 「オンラインで予約できますか？」= Can I book online? Tabelog (食べログ) and OpenTable work in English.

items.script: 英語のメニューはありますか
items.kana: えいごのめにゅーはありますか
romaji: eigo no menyuu wa arimasu ka
items.note: English menus (英語のメニュー) are common in tourist areas but absent in local spots. If there's no English menu: 「写真はありますか？」= Are there photos? (many menus have pictures). 「これは何ですか？」= What is this? Point at the menu. 「おすすめをください」= Please give me your recommendation (safe bet if you trust the chef!). 「おまかせでお願いします」= Please decide for me (chef's choice — says you trust them completely, often gets the best meal).

items.script: 今夜、席は空いていますか
items.kana: こんや、せきはあいていますか
romaji: konya, seki wa aite imasu ka
english: Is there a table available tonight?
items.note: 今夜 (konya) = tonight. 席 (seki) = seat/table. 空いています (aite imasu) = is available/open (from 空く, aku = to become empty). 「今日の夜7時に、二人で予約できますか？」= Can I book for two at 7pm tonight? 「待ちますか？」= Will you wait? (staff may ask this). 「何分くらい待ちますか？」= About how many minutes will I wait? If fully booked: 「満席です」= full/no seats. 「キャンセル待ちはできますか？」= Can I go on the waitlist?

items.script: 何時まで開いていますか
items.kana: なんじまであいていますか
english: Until what time are you open?
items.note: 何時 (nanji) = what time. まで = until. 開いています (aite imasu) = is open. Also: 「何時から開きますか？」= From what time do you open? 「ラストオーダーは何時ですか？」= What time is last orders? (ラストオーダー = last order, very common in Japanese restaurants — they stop taking orders 30-60 min before closing). 「閉店は何時ですか？」= What time does it close? (閉店 = closing).

items.script: どうやって行けばいいですか
items.kana: どうやっていけばいいですか
items.note: どうやって = how / by what means. 行けばいい = should go (〜ばいい = 'it would be good if ~'). Full template: 「〜へはどうやって行けばいいですか？」= How do I get to ~? Means of transport: 電車で (by train), バスで (by bus), タクシーで (by taxi), 歩いて (on foot). After getting directions, confirm: 「〜番の出口から出ればいいですか？」= Should I exit from exit number ~? Tokyo subway exits are numbered and matter a lot!

items.script: 何分くらいかかりますか
items.kana: なんぷんくらいかかりますか
english: About how many minutes does it take?
items.note: 何分 (nanpun) = how many minutes. くらい = about/approximately. かかります = it takes (time/money). 「歩いて何分くらいかかりますか？」= About how many minutes on foot? 「電車で何分ですか？」= How many minutes by train? 「〜から〜まで、どのくらいかかりますか？」= How long does it take from ~ to ~? If the answer is in time: 〜分 (fun/pun) = minutes. 〜時間 (jikan) = hours. 「30分くらいです」= About 30 minutes.

歩いて (aruite) = on foot / walking (て-form of 歩く). 行けますか = can I go? (potential form of 行く). 「ここから歩いて行けますか？」= Can I walk there from here? If yes: 「どのくらいかかりますか？」= How long will it take? 「まっすぐ行って、〜で右/左に曲がってください」= Go straight, then turn right/left at ~. 「信号を渡ってください」= Please cross at the traffic light. 「〜の隣です」= It's next to ~. Japan is a very walkable country — say yes when you can, maps.google.com works great offline.

items.script: 一人でも楽しめますか
items.kana: ひとりでもたのしめますか
english: Is it ok to go solo?
items.note: 一人で (hitori de) = alone. でも = even (emphasises it works even for one person). 楽しめますか = can enjoy? (potential of 楽しむ). Japan is exceptionally solo-travel-friendly: conveyor sushi (回転寿司), ramen counters (カウンター席), solo karaoke (一人カラオケ!), and solo restaurant seats are all common and comfortable. 「一人席はありますか？」= Do you have a solo seat? (Often a counter seat — actually the best seat in many restaurants!)

items.script: 写真を一枚撮っていただけますか
items.kana: しゃしんをいちまいとっていただけますか
romaji: shashin wo ichimai totte itadakemasu ka
english: Could you take a photo of me?
items.note: 一枚 (ichimai) = one sheet (counter for flat things like photos, paper). 撮っていただけますか = could you take for me? (very polite). This is the correct phrase when you want someone to take a photo OF YOU. Compare with: 「写真を撮ってもいいですか？」= May I take a photo (of you/this place)? Hand your phone over after asking — Japanese people are very happy to help and will often take multiple shots to make sure you get a good one!
